speaker: interviewer
text: It's beginning of tape five. It's May 3, 1995, and this is an interview with Lilly Tykocinski.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're getting better with the Tykocinski.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Just a few last things I wanted to touch on-- you had mentioned to me in the pre-interview that you went back to Auschwitz in 1979. Tell me a bit about first, why you took that trip, and what that trip was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just felt that I needed to go. To see where I was. To see where my parents and the rest of the family died. I just felt to-- to fill this void, to be there, you know. I don't have a stone. I don't have a cemetery where to go to. Even they put in-- they're putting up stones, my society, but this is not the place where they were buried though, where they were buried. So I just felt, and my husband felt the same thing, that I want to go back to see the house, to see the cemetery where my grandfather passed away. And I have the-- the picture from the stone. And I went to Auschwitz, and I walked in Auschwitz, and I felt-- not proud of myself, but I felt good that I survived and I can come back to show the Polacks and the Germans that I survived the war. And I'm back to pay tribute to my family that perished there, and to all the six million Jews, wherever they were. I went to Majdanek. I went to Treblinka. I went to all the camps. Just to be there, just to say kaddish for them, just to remember the souls that were gone, that nobody remembers. I says, if I'm still here, I want to go and I want to do that. And it felt good. It felt good. And then I went back in, I think, in 1986 or '85, with my husband's son, and we showed him, and he took pictures. And I explained to him. And I discovered then that in the barracks that we stayed in Birkenau, there was a-- a washroom and a toilet that we never knew about. Because you could not walk around in the barracks. You went up on the perch and you stayed there or you walked out and you went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I opened the door. I says, what's here? I says, I was here four months. I never knew there was a shower, because we never could take a shower. There was outside, where I mentioned before, the [INAUDIBLE] that there was a big toilet. It was boards and holes. And they let you in for a split of a second. So there was a-- a fountain. So I didn't know if I should take the water to drink or to put on my head, because my head was shaved and burned from the sun. The scalp was dry. Or I had to wash my face. So in the split of the second, I did all those three things with a little water. And then when you went into the toilet, as soon as you sit down, she was walking around with a key, and you had to get up. This was-- this was-- I explained to our son how it was, you know, and how all the details-- I could show him the details. How we walked and how the block, it was, and what's-- what's happening. How we slept, crowded and dirty and filthy and the lice business and everything else. As they were growing up, did your children ask you a lot about your wartime experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my house, since my husband was this-- this-- we were talking constantly about the camp. Certain people could not talk about it. I could talk about it. I can talk about it. And I-- I didn't do anything wrong. I was a Jew, and because I was a Jew, I was in concentration camp. And because I was a Jew, my parents-- I lost my mother when-- when I was 12. And my father, that I couldn't say Mama. And I had dear friends, and they had a mother. When I saw her, I just had to-- just to say mother. Because I didn't say mother enough in my life. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why I went back to Auschwitz. I paid homage to them. And and-- and I would go again, if I wouldn't be afraid for the Polacks now. I would go again, now. So I was twice. But now, it's a little bit dangerous. I don't know. They-- they hate us, anyhow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard that people when-- when they marched with the Living March to Auschwitz, I says, I would love to march with them, as the first person with the children of the Living March, to show him that I was there and that I survived. I marched every day to the factory and to home and to factory. And to survive a year and a half in Auschwitz wasn't easy. It wasn't easy at all. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I couldn't cry in Auschwitz. There were no tears because they doped you up. They gave us [INAUDIBLE] into the food. As soon as we came, I think the first week. That you had no tears and no taste and no brains. You couldn't think clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, every time I talk about it, I have to cry. I get very emotional right now, you know? Before, you get so hardened that nothing bothers you anymore. Nothing. Because what we survived-- the end, they take-- and they kidnap somebody for a day. They take you to a psychiatrist. Oh, my God. How will she survive? I says, nobody took me to a psychiatrist after a year and a half in such a death camp. To see the-- the walking deads. To see the death staring into your eyes. Everybody says, oh, you work in the union. You are privileged people. You will be guests. We will be shot dead. You will be guests. That we would be privileged people. That this was our consolation prize, that we work there for-- and here, oh, how will he survive? So it was a different-- was not easy. And why I survived, I don't know. It was just pure luck, or God wanted me to survive. Pure luck. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Is there anything else you wanted to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think we covered it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm, very welcome. I hope someday some of my great-grandchildren will look at it, and they remember maybe, this was my great-granda. And that's the true story. This is not just a book that you read. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Tykocinski, would you introduce your husband for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my husband, Joseph Tykocinski, that I married in 1975. And we will be married 20 years May 11.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 50 years liberated. I'm married 20 years. The first time I was married 18 years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, unless there's anything you want to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do I have to say? [LAUGHS] It's a lot to say. It's not one where there's one word you can say anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to read the letter?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now? Or I figured we would do it with the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, right, that's part of the pictures. OK, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Part of the pictures, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have this letter, that Polish letter, that they-- we sent it to the kids, my mama. This is the copy. It's not easily readable, but I can give you approximately--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, you're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Those are my parents. This is Joel Butnick and Faigla Butnick, from Zawiercie, Poland. The pictures were taken in 1940 as a picture for the kennkarte, for identification. And the pictures survived the camps that my brothers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] carried them through. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] My beautiful mother. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Those are children from Zawiercie that went to a summer camp in 1935. Among them is my cousin and her little brother, here. Her name was Dora Weinreib. But the boy's name, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which row would they be in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle, in the white blouse, in the-- in the second row. Oops. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the picture of children from Zawiercie that attended a summer camp. And in the middle of the second row, in the white blouse, is my cousin Dora Weinreib. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] And on top, the second from left is her little brother, but I forgot their names. Their name. I don't know his name. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Yeah, this is me and my brother Mendel, 1941, in our backyard in front of our house. [PAUSES FOR 23 SECONDS] Those are the pictures of my sister, Hela and her husband. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The picture was taken in 1941. This is her husband, [? Fishel. ?] She was married in 1941, at home. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And this is my sister with her nine-month-old baby. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the baby went to Auschwitz, and my sister died in Auschwitz. They burned in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] That's me, Lilly Butnick. That time, I-- that picture was taken in 1941. I was 13 years old. I had long braids. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was cute. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] That's my sister, Menya, my older sister, Menya She was born in 1925, May 20. And she perished in Auschwitz. She went to death alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: When had this picture been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the war already. Where? At the photographer's place. I don't-- in Zawiercie. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she knew that she's going to death. And she said it, alive to be burned. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And this is my brother, Milton. He was taken to camp in 1940. The picture was taken around probably 1939. And he survived the war in a working camp for five years. And he passed away January 15, 1992. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Those are people, Jews, in my hometown that were arrested. And among them is my father. Why they were arrested, I don't know, but my father was in jail a few weeks, and then we got him out. And my father is the man next to the two Germans on the right side. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] I think this was in the backyard from the police department.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Germans took that photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They are posing with them, so they took the photo. This photograph was left someplace in Poland, you know, after the war. So somebody gave me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were only Jews there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jews, only Jews. Only business men, business people, I think, from the same business. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This is the last letter that my two sisters and my mother wrote to my brothers into camp. In August 26, I dropped them into the mailbox outside the ghetto, and it reached my brothers. And I will read a few words in Polish, and I'll translate to you as much as I can.

speaker: interviewer
text: On which letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left. Yeah. [SPEAKING POLISH] 'My dear children, I'm writing a few words, still from home. We are finished.' [SPEAKING POLISH] 'We have no choice. I hope you will just hold on and survive.' [SPEAKING POLISH] 'Maybe we will see each other sometimes.' [SPEAKING POLISH] 'We cannot help it. This is our destiny. I'm kissing you both. I kiss you. Your mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SPEAKING POLISH] 'I hope and I dream still to see you. We have no choice. But you just keep on living. And I hope you'll survive and take care of yourselves.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SPEAKING POLISH] I cannot get this out. Now, I'm reading the other side, from my sister-- [SPEAKING POLISH] It's very hard to read for me this part-- [SPEAKING POLISH] 'Everything is I'm-- I'm writing and I'm crying. And it's [INAUDIBLE]. And we were-- you were prepared for this. And your brother Leon, maybe he will survive, because he has some chances. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I send you a package last week, and I hope you received it. And I'm crying, and we love you. Hela and Menya.' [PAUSES FOR 19 SECONDS] This is me and my four brothers after the liberation, 1944. We met in Straubing. And I start from the-- that's my oldest brother, Jacob, is on the upper left. And on the right is Mendel, the youngest. And then it's, on the left, Eliazer is the middle one, and then it's Abraham. Jacob and Abraham are still alive. And I, unfortunately, have lost Mendel and Eliazer. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was very happy to have my four brothers after the war. This is the picture of my sister, Menya, and she wears the Jewish armband that we were supposed to wear. And it was made in 1941, at home. This dress, with the polka dots, became official summer dress for the-- for the halflings, for the campers in Auschwitz. I came and my sister came with the same dresses. And everybody-- whoever had a navy dress with white polka dots were wearing in camp, like, in the summer. They collected all the dresses. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zawiercie someplace, in a garden. I don't know where she went, who took it. Yeah. This is the picture of my family, my husband and my two children. It was Harvey's bar mitzvah, and we are entering the ballroom. The happy mother with the two sons and my husband, Seymour Robbins. And it took place in the Forest Hills Jewish Center, the bar mitzvah. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] OK. This is the picture with my brothers at my son's bar mitzvah. My brother, Eliazer, came from Germany to be with us. And I was a very happy sister to have my brothers there with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name them, from left to right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Jacob is the far left, and Abraham, and then Milton, and Mendel is the far right. And Josef is-- oh gosh-- And Eliazer is next to me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1979, in June. This is me, Lilly Tykocinski Butnick. And this is the block that I spent four miserable months. Block 20, from [INAUDIBLE] in Birkenau. And this is the piece of wood that I slept for four months. That I stayed up. I don't know if I slept there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to the left, it shows the entrance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It show the entrance to the block. Block 20. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] And this is the inside of the block. And this is the-- the bed, the comfort that we had. I slept on top. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And there was a window, which was broken. It was raining in. But at least we saw the daylight.
